result.markdown:
---
title: "Day 4 Activity"
output: html
embed-resources: TRUE
execute: 
  echo: FALSE
---



# Activity: Your First Quarto Report

You'll build a short Quarto report from scratch. Take 20 minutes for this activity.

**Setup:**

1. Open RStudio.
2. `File > New File > Quarto Document...`
3. Title: "**Penguin Exploration**", Author: your name.
4. Pick **HTML** as the format.
5. **Uncheck** "Use visual markdown editor" if you want to see the raw source.
6. Click **Create**.

You'll get a template document, potentially with example content. **Delete anything below the YAML header if present.** Keep the YAML.

**Step 1 - Save the file**

`File > Save As...`: call it `penguin-report.qmd`, save somewhere you'll know where to find it. 

**Step 2 - Save the provided image**

Place your downloaded `fig-mass-histogram-1.png` in the **same folder** where you saved `penguin-report.qmd`. 

**Step 3 - Upgrade your YAML**

We can make the HTML output look professional right away. Modify the top of your document (between the `---` lines) to add a Table of Contents (`toc`) and a custom theme. **Be careful with indentation (use spaces instead of tabs) since YAML can be picky**.

````markdown
---
title: "Penguin Exploration"
author: "Your Name"
format:
  html:
    toc: TRUE
      theme: litera
embed-resources: TRUE
---
````

Be sure to include `embed-resources: TRUE` to ensure Quarto packages the entire document (CSS styles, JavaScript libraries, fonts, images) into one, self-contained HTML file. This enables offline viewing of the document and ensures that all content you create will be saved in the html file you submit on Canvas. 

DON'T FORGET: *Render and open your html file to see if it worked.*

**Step 4 - Add a setup chunk**

Insert a code chunk (`Ctrl + Alt + I` on Windows or `Cmd + Option + I on Mac). Make it look like this:

````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: setup
#| include: FALSE

library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
```
````
DON'T FORGET: *Render and open your html file to see if it worked.*

**Step 5 - Write an intro with a link, an image, and inline code**

Below the setup chunk, add a heading and a paragraph.

Notice three new Markdown features here:

1. **Images:** Formatted as `![caption](path/to/image)`. We're pulling the package hex logo straight from the web and using Quarto's `{width}` and `fig-align` attributes to float it to the right.
2. **Hyperlinks:** Formatted as `[text](url)`
3. **Inline code:** The backticks around `` `r nrow(penguins)` `` will automatically calculate the number of rows in the dataset when you render.

Copy and paste this into your document:

``` markdown
# Introduction

![]([https://allisonhorst.github.io/palmerpenguins/logo.png](https://allisonhorst.github.io/palmerpenguins/logo.png)){width="120" fig-align="right"}

This report explores the [**Palmer Penguins**](https://allisonhorst.github.io/palmerpenguins/) 
data set, which contains measurements on 344 penguins from 
three islands in Antarctica.
```

DON'T FORGET: *Render and open your html file to see if it worked.*

**Step 6 - Add a plot**

Copy and paste the code below to insert appropriate headings and another chunk (see if you can remember the keyboard shortcut for adding a chunk.) Then put a histogram in your new chunk. We are adding a fig-cap to label the figure, which lets us automatically reference it in our text.

``` markdown
# Exploratory Data Analysis

## Basic Histogram

![Distribution of penguin body mass](fig-mass-histogram-1.png){#fig-mass-histogram width=672}
```

DON'T FORGET: *Render and open your html file to see if it worked.*

**Step 7 - Add commentary**

Below the plot, write 2-3 sentences (in Markdown) describing what you see. Include this exact text somewhere in your paragraph to see how Quarto handles cross-references:

``` markdown
As shown in @fig-mass-histogram, the data is...
```
DON'T FORGET: *Render and open your html file to see if it worked.*

**Step 8 - Add a table**

Copy and paste the code below to add a new section heading and a code chunk that uses the `kable()` function from the `knitr` package to draw a clean table of the first 5 rows:

``` markdown
## Data Preview


::: {#tbl-preview .cell tbl-cap='First 5 rows of the penguins dataset'}
::: {.cell-output-display}


|species |island    | bill_length_mm| bill_depth_mm| flipper_length_mm| body_mass_g|sex    | year|
|:-------|:---------|--------------:|-------------:|-----------------:|-----------:|:------|----:|
|Adelie  |Torgersen |           39.1|          18.7|               181|        3750|male   | 2007|
|Adelie  |Torgersen |           39.5|          17.4|               186|        3800|female | 2007|
|Adelie  |Torgersen |           40.3|          18.0|               195|        3250|female | 2007|
|Adelie  |Torgersen |             NA|            NA|                NA|          NA|NA     | 2007|
|Adelie  |Torgersen |           36.7|          19.3|               193|        3450|female | 2007|


:::
:::
```

DON'T FORGET: *Render and open your html file to see if it worked.*

**Step 9 - Final render**

Press Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K. Look at the HTML file that pops up. Notice your table of contents, your styled theme, the evaluated inline code, and the figure numbering.

If you finish early, try one or more of these:

- **Create a Tabset**: Wrap two heading and code chunks in a tabset block by copying and pasting the code below to hide multiple plots behind clickable tabs:

``` markdown
::: {.panel-tabset}

### Mass Plot
(Put your mass plot code chunk here)

### Flipper Plot
(Put a new flipper_length_mm plot code chunk here)

:::
```
- **Change the Output**: Try changing `format: html` to `format: docx` in the YAML and re-render to get a Word document. (Note: themes and tabsets are HTML-only).
- **Hide the Code**: Set `echo: FALSE` in your YAML under `execute:` to hide all code from the output, leaving only the text, plots, and tables.

``` markdown
---
title: "Penguin Exploration"
author: "Your Name"
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: litera
execute:
  echo: false
---
```

**To turn in:** Save your `.qmd` file and the rendered HTML. We'll check back in at the end of class.
